items.passage: A steam engine is a machine that converts the thermal energy of pressurized steam into mechanical motion. Early versions, built to pump water from deep mines, burned huge amounts of coal for little work. The chief fault lay in the single cylinder, which was heated by incoming steam and then cooled by water on every stroke. [■] Each cycle thus wasted most of its fuel reheating metal that the previous stroke had chilled. This heating and cooling was the obstacle the engineer James Watt set out to remove. In the 1760s he added a separate condenser, a chamber where steam could be cooled without cooling the main cylinder. [■] The cylinder could now stay hot from stroke to stroke, so far less coal was needed to raise fresh steam. [■] Watt later linked the piston to a rotating wheel, letting the engine turn machinery rather than merely pump. This rotary motion transformed the engine from a mine pump into the driving force of mills and, eventually, locomotives. [■] Workshops that had depended on flowing rivers could now be built wherever coal was available, freeing manufacturers from fixed water sources. However, the machines stayed heavy and burned fuel steadily, so they suited large enterprises far better than small workshops that could not justify the expense.
paragraphs: A steam engine is a machine that converts the thermal energy of pressurized steam into mechanical motion. Early versions, built to pump water from deep mines, burned huge amounts of coal for little work. The chief fault lay in the single cylinder, which was heated by incoming steam and then cooled by water on every stroke. [■] Each cycle thus wasted most of its fuel reheating metal that the previous stroke had chilled.

This heating and cooling was the obstacle the engineer James Watt set out to remove. In the 1760s he added a separate condenser, a chamber where steam could be cooled without cooling the main cylinder. [■] The cylinder could now stay hot from stroke to stroke, so far less coal was needed to raise fresh steam. [■] Watt later linked the piston to a rotating wheel, letting the engine turn machinery rather than merely pump.

This rotary motion transformed the engine from a mine pump into the driving force of mills and, eventually, locomotives. [■] Workshops that had depended on flowing rivers could now be built wherever coal was available, freeing manufacturers from fixed water sources. However, the machines stayed heavy and burned fuel steadily, so they suited large enterprises far better than small workshops that could not justify the expense.

questions.stem: The word "chief" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: frequent
options.B: principal
options.C: hidden
options.D: technical

questions.explanation: The "chief fault" is the main or principal problem, the single flaw the paragraph goes on to explain.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what did the separate condenser make possible?

options.A: It allowed the cylinder to be cooled far faster after each stroke.
options.B: It removed the need for coal as a fuel altogether.
options.C: It kept the main cylinder hot, so much less fuel was required.
options.D: It raised the pressure of the steam entering the cylinder.

questions.explanation: The passage states the cylinder "could now stay hot from one stroke to the next, so far less coal was needed to raise fresh steam."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about small workshops?

options.A: They were the first businesses to install the new engines.
options.B: They preferred to move closer to available coal supplies.
options.C: They abandoned water power far more quickly than large mills did.
options.D: They mostly continued to rely on their older sources of power.

questions.explanation: Because the engines "suited large enterprises far better than the small workshops that could not justify their expense," such workshops would keep using older power.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention workshops that had depended on flowing rivers in paragraph 3?

options.A: To illustrate how steam power freed factories from fixed locations.
options.B: To argue that water power was more reliable than steam power.
options.C: To show that steam engines were less flexible than waterwheels.
options.D: To describe the particular rivers used by early textile mills.

questions.explanation: The example shows manufacturers being "freed from fixed water sources," illustrating the geographic freedom steam provided.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'And because the condenser let the cylinder reuse rather than waste that heat, a Watt engine could perform the same pumping task on a fraction of the coal its predecessor required.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1
options.B: Location 2
options.C: Location 3
options.D: Location 4

questions.explanation: The sentence elaborates the fuel savings just stated ("so far less coal was needed to raise fresh steam"), making it the concrete follow-up at Location 3.

items.passage: Buzz pollination, also known as sonication, is a process in which certain bees vibrate their bodies to shake loose pollen that a flower keeps tightly enclosed. In many flowering plants, the pollen sits inside tube-shaped anthers that open only through a minute pore at the very tip. [■] Ordinary contact, the means by which most pollen passes between blossoms, cannot draw these hidden grains out. This concealment explains why such plants depend on a specialized behavior rather than on passive contact. A visiting bee anchors its jaws onto the anther and rapidly contracts its flight muscles without beating its wings, producing vibrations of a particular frequency. [■] The shaking travels through the anther and drives a cloud of pollen out through the narrow pore, dusting the insect's body. [■] This mechanism matters because several major crops, among them tomatoes, blueberries, and eggplants, hold their pollen in exactly this way. Honeybees, the most familiar of pollinators, cannot sonicate, so growers increasingly rely on bumblebees and other wild species to set fruit. [■] However, the wild bees capable of buzzing are declining across many regions, and their loss could quietly reduce harvests long before the cause became obvious.
paragraphs: Buzz pollination, also known as sonication, is a process in which certain bees vibrate their bodies to shake loose pollen that a flower keeps tightly enclosed. In many flowering plants, the pollen sits inside tube-shaped anthers that open only through a minute pore at the very tip. [■] Ordinary contact, the means by which most pollen passes between blossoms, cannot draw these hidden grains out.

This concealment explains why such plants depend on a specialized behavior rather than on passive contact. A visiting bee anchors its jaws onto the anther and rapidly contracts its flight muscles without beating its wings, producing vibrations of a particular frequency. [■] The shaking travels through the anther and drives a cloud of pollen out through the narrow pore, dusting the insect's body. [■]

This mechanism matters because several major crops, among them tomatoes, blueberries, and eggplants, hold their pollen in exactly this way. Honeybees, the most familiar of pollinators, cannot sonicate, so growers increasingly rely on bumblebees and other wild species to set fruit. [■] However, the wild bees capable of buzzing are declining across many regions, and their loss could quietly reduce harvests long before the cause became obvious.

questions.stem: The word "concealment" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: attraction
options.B: hiding
options.C: release
options.D: arrangement

questions.explanation: "This concealment" refers back to pollen that is kept enclosed and hidden inside the anther, so it means hiding.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, how does a bee release the enclosed pollen?

options.A: By beating its wings rapidly against the anther's pore.
options.B: By biting through the wall of the anther to reach the pollen.
options.C: By warming the anther until its narrow pore widens.
options.D: By vibrating its flight muscles while gripping the anther.

questions.explanation: The bee "contracts its flight muscles without beating its wings," and the resulting vibration drives the pollen out.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true EXCEPT

options.A: Some flowers keep their pollen inside tube-shaped anthers.
options.B: Bumblebees are increasingly used to pollinate certain crops.
options.C: Honeybees release pollen by sonicating tube-shaped anthers.
options.D: The vibrations force pollen out through a small pore.

questions.explanation: The passage states plainly that "Honeybees ... cannot sonicate," so the claim that they do is not supported.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about crops such as tomatoes and blueberries?

options.A: Their productivity depends on the presence of buzz-pollinating bees.
options.B: They can be pollinated successfully by honeybees on their own.
options.C: They have recently begun to attract entirely new pollinators.
options.D: They are grown mainly in regions that lack any wild bees.

questions.explanation: Since these crops hold pollen that only sonicating bees can release and honeybees cannot buzz, their fruiting depends on buzz-pollinating bees.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'For example, commercial tomato growers now keep bumblebee colonies inside their greenhouses, where honeybees would otherwise leave the flowers unpollinated.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1
options.B: Location 2
options.C: Location 3
options.D: Location 4

questions.explanation: The example follows directly from the claim that growers "rely on bumblebees and other wild species to set fruit," so it fits best at Location 4.

items.passage: A river delta is a landform that develops where a river deposits its sediment upon entering a calmer body of water, such as a sea or a lake. Deltas take shape only where the river supplies particles faster than waves and currents can sweep them away. [■] Where that balance holds, the gathering sediment builds outward into the triangular fan for which the feature is named. This outward growth depends on a simple change in the moving water's energy. As the channel meets the standing water and slows, it can no longer transport its load, dropping the heaviest sand near the mouth and carrying the finest clay farthest offshore. [■] The main channel repeatedly splits into smaller branches, called distributaries, that spread the sediment across a broad front. [■] These fertile, level plains, some of the most crowded places on Earth, have long attracted dense human settlement and intensive farming. Yet the same deltas are unusually fragile: because they sit barely above sea level, they sink whenever the supply of fresh sediment is interrupted. [■] Dams built far upstream now trap sediment in their reservoirs, and many of these deltas are eroding and subsiding faster than the weakened rivers can rebuild them.
paragraphs: A river delta is a landform that develops where a river deposits its sediment upon entering a calmer body of water, such as a sea or a lake. Deltas take shape only where the river supplies particles faster than waves and currents can sweep them away. [■] Where that balance holds, the gathering sediment builds outward into the triangular fan for which the feature is named.

This outward growth depends on a simple change in the moving water's energy. As the channel meets the standing water and slows, it can no longer transport its load, dropping the heaviest sand near the mouth and carrying the finest clay farthest offshore. [■] The main channel repeatedly splits into smaller branches, called distributaries, that spread the sediment across a broad front. [■]

These fertile, level plains, some of the most crowded places on Earth, have long attracted dense human settlement and intensive farming. Yet the same deltas are unusually fragile: because they sit barely above sea level, they sink whenever the supply of fresh sediment is interrupted. [■] Dams built far upstream now trap sediment in their reservoirs, and many of these deltas are eroding and subsiding faster than the weakened rivers can rebuild them.

questions.stem: The word "fragile" in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to

options.A: valuable
options.B: vulnerable
options.C: temporary
options.D: remote

questions.explanation: The deltas are called fragile because they "sink whenever the supply of fresh sediment is interrupted," meaning they are easily harmed, or vulnerable.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what happens as the river's current slows?

options.A: It picks up additional sediment from the seabed.
options.B: It reverses its direction during periods of high tide.
options.C: It carves a much deeper channel near the river's mouth.
options.D: It drops coarse particles first and fine particles farther out.

questions.explanation: The slowing water drops "the heaviest sand near the mouth" and carries "the finest clay farthest offshore."

questions.stem: What does "These fertile, level plains" refer to in paragraph 3?

options.A: the deltas built up from deposited sediment
options.B: the reservoirs held behind the upstream dams
options.C: the waves and currents lying offshore
options.D: the branching channels called distributaries

questions.explanation: The phrase refers back to the deltas, the flat land formed as the river spreads its sediment across a broad front.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about dams built far upstream?

options.A: They help deltas grow by storing water for the river.
options.B: They were built mainly to protect deltas from flooding.
options.C: They can speed the erosion of deltas lying downstream.
options.D: They have stopped most rivers from reaching the sea.

questions.explanation: Because upstream dams "trap sediment," starving the delta of the material it needs, they hasten erosion and subsidence downstream.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'For instance, someone wading seaward from the river's mouth would cross first the coarse sand, then finer silt, and at last a smooth floor of clay.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1
options.B: Location 2
options.C: Location 3
options.D: Location 4

questions.explanation: The sentence gives a concrete picture of the size sorting just described, coarse sand near the mouth and fine clay offshore, so it fits at Location 2.

items.passage: Tonality is the system of organizing Western music around a single central pitch, known as the tonic, toward which the other notes seem to lean. Within a given key, this tonic anchors the entire passage, giving listeners a clear sense of a musical home. The remaining pitches of the scale are heard in relation to it, some sounding restful and others generating a pull that seeks release. This pull arises most strongly from the way chords are built and ordered. A chord constructed on the fifth note of the scale, the dominant, contains tones that clash mildly with one another and sound unresolved. Listeners feel this instability as a demand for motion, and the dominant characteristically gives way to the tonic, a shift that the ear accepts as a satisfying arrival. This expectation is something composers have long manipulated to shape emotion. By delaying the return to the tonic, a composer can stretch tension across many measures, making the eventual resolution feel hard-won. However, by the early twentieth century some composers deliberately abandoned any single tonic, arguing that centuries of tonal habit had begun to limit rather than enable fresh expression.
paragraphs: Tonality is the system of organizing Western music around a single central pitch, known as the tonic, toward which the other notes seem to lean. Within a given key, this tonic anchors the entire passage, giving listeners a clear sense of a musical home. The remaining pitches of the scale are heard in relation to it, some sounding restful and others generating a pull that seeks release.

This pull arises most strongly from the way chords are built and ordered. A chord constructed on the fifth note of the scale, the dominant, contains tones that clash mildly with one another and sound unresolved. Listeners feel this instability as a demand for motion, and the dominant characteristically gives way to the tonic, a shift that the ear accepts as a satisfying arrival.

This expectation is something composers have long manipulated to shape emotion. By delaying the return to the tonic, a composer can stretch tension across many measures, making the eventual resolution feel hard-won. However, by the early twentieth century some composers deliberately abandoned any single tonic, arguing that centuries of tonal habit had begun to limit rather than enable fresh expression.

questions.stem: The word "anchors" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: opens
options.B: secures
options.C: shortens
options.D: decorates

questions.explanation: The tonic anchors the passage by "giving listeners a clear sense of a musical home," that is, it steadies or secures it.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what gives the dominant chord its unstable quality?

options.A: It is built on the very first note of the scale.
options.B: It is played more loudly than the tonic chord is.
options.C: It combines tones that clash and sound unresolved.
options.D: It borrows several notes from a different key.

questions.explanation: The dominant "contains tones that clash mildly with one another and sound unresolved," producing its instability.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about delaying the return to the tonic?

options.A: It heightens the listener's satisfaction when resolution finally arrives.
options.B: It weakens the sense of tension that a passage can build.
options.C: It shortens the overall length of a musical composition.
options.D: It removes the need for a tonic chord altogether.

questions.explanation: Delay "stretch[es] tension across many measures, making the eventual resolution feel hard-won," implying a stronger payoff when it arrives.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention composers of the early twentieth century in paragraph 3?

options.A: To identify the musicians who first defined the tonic.
options.B: To describe the specific chords favored after the year 1900.
options.C: To prove that tonality steadily grew more and more popular over time.
options.D: To show that the tonal system was eventually challenged and set aside.

questions.explanation: These composers "deliberately abandoned any single tonic," showing that the tonal system was later rejected, a limitation move.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true EXCEPT

options.A: The tonic acts as a central note within a musical key.
options.B: The dominant chord tends to give way to the tonic.
options.C: Delaying resolution reduces the emotional tension of a piece.
options.D: Some later composers rejected the use of a single tonic.

questions.explanation: The passage says delay "stretch[es] tension," the opposite of reducing it, so this statement is not supported.

items.passage: Globalization is the process through which economies, cultures, and populations across the world become increasingly connected by the movement of goods, people, and ideas. In its earliest form, this connection ran along slow overland and maritime routes that carried silk, spices, and precious metals between distant regions. Such exchange touched only a thin layer of society, since transport was costly and confined largely to rare, high-value luxuries. These early connections deepened dramatically during the nineteenth century, when steamships, railways, and the telegraph slashed the time and cost of moving both cargo and information. Trade widened to bulk goods such as grain and coal, yet the new system was uneven, tied closely to colonial empires, and it broke down amid the world wars and the economic depression of the early twentieth century. This expanded system took firmer hold after the mid-twentieth century, when standardized shipping containers and, later, digital communication made worldwide exchange faster and far cheaper than ever before. Everyday manufactured goods now cross whole oceans routinely. However, this dense integration also transmits shocks quickly, so a disruption in one region can ripple through the entire global network within days.
paragraphs: Globalization is the process through which economies, cultures, and populations across the world become increasingly connected by the movement of goods, people, and ideas. In its earliest form, this connection ran along slow overland and maritime routes that carried silk, spices, and precious metals between distant regions. Such exchange touched only a thin layer of society, since transport was costly and confined largely to rare, high-value luxuries.

These early connections deepened dramatically during the nineteenth century, when steamships, railways, and the telegraph slashed the time and cost of moving both cargo and information. Trade widened to bulk goods such as grain and coal, yet the new system was uneven, tied closely to colonial empires, and it broke down amid the world wars and the economic depression of the early twentieth century.

This expanded system took firmer hold after the mid-twentieth century, when standardized shipping containers and, later, digital communication made worldwide exchange faster and far cheaper than ever before. Everyday manufactured goods now cross whole oceans routinely. However, this dense integration also transmits shocks quickly, so a disruption in one region can ripple through the entire global network within days.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: The role of steamships in nineteenth-century trade.
options.B: The stages through which global connections have deepened over time.
options.C: The kinds of goods carried along the earliest trade routes.
options.D: The reasons world trade collapsed in the early twentieth century.

questions.explanation: The passage traces globalization from slow early routes, through nineteenth-century expansion, to modern container shipping, describing successive stages of deepening connection.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, what characterized the earliest form of global exchange?

options.A: It moved large volumes of ordinary goods cheaply between distant continents.
options.B: It was controlled by one especially powerful empire.
options.C: It was slow, costly, and limited mostly to luxury goods.
options.D: It relied mainly on overland routes crossing Europe.

questions.explanation: The passage says early exchange "was costly and confined largely to rare, high-value luxuries."

questions.stem: How does paragraph 2 relate to paragraph 1?

options.A: It restates the main argument of paragraph 1 using fresh examples.
options.B: It offers evidence that contradicts the claims of paragraph 1.
options.C: It defines several terms that paragraph 1 had left unexplained.
options.D: It advances the timeline to a wider but still unstable phase.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 moves the chronology forward to nineteenth-century expansion that was broader yet "uneven" and eventually broke down, continuing the staged history.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about shipping containers and digital communication?

options.A: They were developed largely to serve military supply needs.
options.B: They erased the remaining differences between national economies.
options.C: They made global trade far slower and more expensive than ever before.
options.D: They let goods and information circulate more cheaply than in earlier eras.

questions.explanation: These innovations "made worldwide exchange faster and far cheaper than ever before," implying wider, cheaper circulation than in earlier phases.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what happened to the expanded trade of the nineteenth century?

options.A: It collapsed during years of war and economic crisis.
options.B: It grew steadily without interruption right up to the present.
options.C: It shifted almost entirely onto overland railway routes.
options.D: It was gradually replaced by local self-sufficiency.

questions.explanation: The passage states the system "broke down amid the world wars and the economic depression of the early twentieth century."
